one_line_takeaway: Semantic LLM caching (GPTCache-style and semantic KV caches like SemShareKV) is shown to be inherently collision-prone because its locality-preserving embedding-based key matching directly conflicts with the avalanche-effect property needed for collision resistance, and the paper's CacheAttack framework exploits this via GCG-style adversarial suffixes plus a latency-based cache-hit validator to hijack LLM responses (up to ~87% hit rate) and agent tool invocations, including a financial-agent case study causing an unauthorized SELL order.
key_finding: Response hijacking (RQ1, Table 1): on GPTCache-style semantic cache, CacheAttack-1 achieves HR 86.9% / ISR 81.1%, CacheAttack-2 achieves HR 83.1% / ISR 77.1%, versus the GA baseline's HR 36.2% / ISR 33.7% and a 0.0/0.0 clean baseline; on semantic KV cache, CacheAttack-1 HR 85.8% / ISR 83.0%, CacheAttack-2 HR 80.4% / ISR 77.0%, versus GA HR 34.0% / ISR 31.5%. On production services (Table 2): CacheAttack-1/2 achieve HR 80.6%/78.2% on AWS Bedrock and 88.3%/86.7% on Azure API Management, versus GA's 12.4%/15.2%. Agent tool-invocation hijacking (RQ2, Table 3): using CacheAttack-2, hit rate rises from 90.6% (semantic cache, benign) to 93.2% (attack, delta 8.7pp) and 87.1% to 93.2% (semantic KV cache, delta 13.1pp); tool selection rate (TSR) drops by up to 3.2pp (semantic cache) / 7.9pp (semantic KV cache) and answer accuracy (Acc) drops as much as 83.8% under attack on semantic cache and 79.1% on semantic KV cache (the paper states the attack 'decreases up to 84.5% of TSR and 83.8% of Acc,' referring to the benign->attack magnitudes, not absolute post-attack values). Transferability (RQ3, Table 4): across four sentence-embedding surrogates (MiniLM, GTE, E5, BGE), diagonal (white-box, gs=gt) hit rates all exceed 92% (93.4/92.1/94.0/93.1%); off-diagonal cross-model transfer is uneven and correlates with architectural/training similarity (e.g., MiniLM->GTE 78.6% vs. MiniLM->BGE 49.8%). Case study (RQ4): a financial agent (Qwen3-32B + GPTCache) with a set_order(stock, quantity, BUY/SELL) tool is shown, via a two-stage plant-then-collide attack, to execute an unauthorized SELL order for a victim who asked only for general investment advice. Defenses (Section 7): key salting reduces HR/ISR by at most 21.0pp/25.4pp (semantic cache, template salting) and 10.8pp/20.2pp (semantic KV cache) -- described by the authors as a partial mitigation, not a full defense; perplexity screening at insertion time is proposed but evaluated only via PPL-distribution comparison against Natural Questions, not as a hit-rate reduction; per-user cache isolation eliminates cross-user collision but is argued (not separately quantified in the excerpted text) to reduce cache hit rate and raise backend LLM invocation cost/latency, reproducing the same performance-security trade-off the paper's theory predicts.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from cited related work on prompt-cache privacy leakage/side channels) is threefold: (1) it formalizes LLM semantic cache keys as locality-preserving fuzzy hashes and proves (Lemma 3.1) a lower bound on false-positive cache hit probability, arguing an inherent trade-off between locality (needed for cache-hit performance) and the avalanche-effect collision resistance needed for security; (2) it proposes CacheAttack, described as the first black-box key-collision attack framework against semantic caching, with two variants -- CacheAttack-1 (validates directly against the black-box target model, but slow due to a mandatory TTL wait between iterations and easily detected via traffic analysis) and CacheAttack-2 (uses a surrogate embedding model for high-throughput candidate filtering, invoking the black-box target only for final verification); (3) it empirically evaluates the attack on LLM response hijacking, agent tool-invocation hijacking, cross-embedding-model transferability, a financial-agent case study, and three candidate defenses (key salting, perplexity screening, per-user cache isolation).
threat_model: Attacker sends arbitrary prompts to the LLM system and observes responses (black-box access to the pipeline). The embedding model computing the cache key is a black-box function to the attacker (parameters, vector representations, similarity threshold all inaccessible). The attacker can use a local surrogate embedding model (e.g., BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5) to optimize adversarial prompts and infer cache hit/miss from observable signals (response consistency, recurring generation patterns, end-to-end latency variation). The attacker does not modify existing cached values, does not access internal KV representations, does not control cache eviction policy, and does not alter embedding-model or backend-LLM parameters -- all attacks occur at inference time and exploit the cache's locality property. Attack goal: craft an adversarial prompt whose semantic cache key collides with a victim's benign query key so the victim is served an attacker-planted malicious response.
limitations: The paper's own text frames per-user isolation's cost impact qualitatively rather than with a measured hit-rate/latency delta in the reviewed sections. Perplexity screening is evaluated by comparing PPL distributions rather than by directly measuring its effect on attack hit rate/ISR, so its practical mitigation strength versus CacheAttack is not directly quantified the same way key salting is. The evaluation of production services (AWS Bedrock, Azure API Management) is limited to Hit Rate only (no ISR reported in Table 2), and defense evaluation (salting, isolation) is not repeated against those two production services. The threat model assumes an attacker can repeatedly query the system and observe latency without being blocked, and that a usable surrogate embedding model exists; robustness against systems that add latency-obfuscation or rate limiting is not evaluated in the sections reviewed. Attacker also needs the ability to first plant a malicious entry into the shared multi-tenant cache (implicit precondition for the response-hijacking and financial-agent attacks).

datasets_used: SC-IPI: a curated dataset of 4,185 Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) instructions across security-critical scenarios, generated using GPT-5.2 (introduced by this paper)
Natural Questions (NQ) -- 50 benign prompts sampled as benign victim queries; also used as PPL baseline for the perplexity-screening defense
A custom agentic tool-use dataset built on the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) for RQ2 tool-invocation hijacking
quantitative_results: RQ1 semantic cache: CacheAttack-1 HR 86.9%/ISR 81.1%; CacheAttack-2 HR 83.1%/ISR 77.1%; GA baseline HR 36.2%/ISR 33.7%; Clean 0.0%/0.0%
RQ1 semantic KV cache: CacheAttack-1 HR 85.8%/ISR 83.0%; CacheAttack-2 HR 80.4%/ISR 77.0%; GA baseline HR 34.0%/ISR 31.5%
RQ1 production services: AWS Bedrock HR -- CacheAttack-1 80.6%, CacheAttack-2 78.2%, GA 12.4%, Clean 0.0%; Azure HR -- CacheAttack-1 88.3%, CacheAttack-2 86.7%, GA 15.2%, Clean 0.0%

RQ2 semantic cache: HR benign 90.6% -> attack 93.2% (delta 8.7pp); TSR benign 84.5% -> attack 87.0% (delta 3.2pp, note: table lists 87.0 under attack column with Acc 83.8%); Acc drop reported as up to 83.8%
RQ2 semantic KV cache: HR benign 87.1% -> attack 93.2% (delta 13.1pp); TSR benign 80.1% -> attack 87.0% (delta 7.9pp); Acc reported at 79.1%

RQ3 transferability (Table 4, Hit Rate %): diagonal (white-box) MiniLM 93.4, GTE 92.1, E5 94.0, BGE 93.1; example cross-model transfers MiniLM->GTE 78.6, MiniLM->E5 54.2, MiniLM->BGE 49.8, E5->BGE 81.7, BGE->E5 86.9

Defense -- key salting (Table 5, reduction in percentage points): Prefix salting semantic cache dHR 19.2/dISR 25.4, semantic KV cache dHR 10.8/dISR 20.2; Suffix salting dHR 9.5/dISR 10.2 (semantic cache), dHR 8.3/dISR 9.1 (semantic KV cache); Template salting dHR 21.0/dISR 24.8 (semantic cache), dHR 10.7/dISR 19.8 (semantic KV cache); Best overall dHR 21.0/dISR 25.4 (semantic cache), dHR 10.8/dISR 20.2 (semantic KV cache)
baselines_compared: Genetic Algorithm (GA) black-box generator baseline (Lambora et al., 2019), using cosine-similarity fitness with the same latency-based validator as CacheAttack
Clean/Benign condition (no attack) as a 0% hit-rate floor
